common_name: Cape York Lily (Aussie Plume, Native Turmeric)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in part shade to dappled shade; Cape York lily is an Australian rainforest curcuma adapted to warm, humid shade conditions and produces a stunning, upright plume of white, pink, or lilac-purple bracts in summer — one of the most ornamental of all gingers.

directions: Plant rhizome sections 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in part shade; allow rhizomes to dry and go dormant in winter. Curcuma australasica (Cape York lily or Aussie plume) is an Australian native curcuma — one of very few curcuma species native outside of South and Southeast Asia — found in the rainforests of Cape York Peninsula in far north Queensland. It produces attractive, broadly lance-shaped, medium-green leaves and upright, compact flower 'cones' 10–14 inches tall composed of overlapping, overlapping, boat-shaped bracts in white, pink-tipped white, or lilac-purple, with tiny, cream-yellow true flowers emerging between the bracts. The Australian origin gives it slightly different cultural requirements from Asian curcumas — it prefers the seasonal rhythm of a wet summer and dry winter characteristic of tropical Queensland. As a cut flower, the inflorescences last 2–3 weeks in a vase. In zone 9–12 shade gardens, it thrives in the same conditions as other gingers: moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in dappled shade. A lovely, rarely-grown species for ginger collectors and tropical garden enthusiasts.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Gingers

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil in part shade to full sun; variegated shell ginger is more shade-tolerant than common shell ginger and the variegated foliage is brightest in morning sun with afternoon shade — full shade produces less crisp variegation but plants still thrive.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in part shade to full sun; provide morning sun for best foliage color. Alpinia vittata (variegated shell ginger) is a Pacific Island native ginger producing one of the most striking foliage displays of any tropical plant — upright, 3-to-5-foot stems with long (18-inch), lance-shaped leaves dramatically striped with alternating bands of green and creamy yellow, creating a bold, bright, bamboo-like architectural effect. Unlike common shell ginger (A. zerumbet) which is grown for both foliage and flowers, variegated shell ginger is primarily a foliage plant — the striped leaves are the main ornamental feature, providing year-round tropical color in shade and part-shade situations. The shell ginger flowers (nodding racemes of white and pink flowers resembling porcelain shells) are less freely produced than in the straight species. In zone 8–12, the plant persists as a perennial; the above-ground parts may die back in a hard freeze but the rhizomes survive in the ground with mulch protection. An outstanding tropical foliage specimen for part-shade situations in Gulf Coast gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Gingers
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Plants

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; CAUTION — white wandering jew is invasive in Florida, the Gulf South, parts of California, and many tropical and warm-temperate regions where it forms dense mats that exclude native understory plants — verify local regulations before planting.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile soil in shade to part shade; CAUTION — invasive in Florida and warm-temperate regions; never plant near natural areas. Tradescantia fluminensis (small leaf spiderwort or white wandering jew) is a South American succulent trailing perennial closely related to the more commonly grown 'wandering Jew' (T. zebrina) and tradescantia group. This species is smaller-leaved with glossy, oval, bright-green leaves on trailing stems, producing tiny white flowers. It is widely used as a shade groundcover indoors and in warm gardens for its easy growth, shade tolerance, and rapid spreading habit. However, T. fluminensis is classified as an invasive weed in Florida, South Africa, New Zealand, Hawaii, and other warm regions where it escapes cultivation and forms dense, impenetrable mats in woodland understories, smothering native understory vegetation. In New Zealand, it is considered one of the most damaging environmental weeds in coastal forest understories. Where legal and appropriate (i.e., in fully contained indoor or urban settings far from natural areas), it is an effective low-maintenance shade groundcover. Note: causes skin irritation in some individuals. Grow with caution and responsibility.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Invasive Plants
New Zealand Department of Conservation — Invasive Plants

common_name: Turtle Vine (Creeping Inch Plant, Miniature Inch Plant)

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings in spring in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained potting mix or garden soil in part shade to full shade; turtle vine is a tiny-leaved, trailing shade plant from Central and South America that is outstanding for hanging baskets, terrariums, vivarium substrate, and shaded groundcover in warm, humid climates.

directions: Plant stem cuttings in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral potting mix or garden soil in shade; keep consistently moist. Callisia repens (turtle vine or creeping inch plant) is a tiny-leaved, mat-forming, trailing succulent from Central and South America in the Commelinaceae family — related to tradescantias and wandering Jews — producing dense, low, creeping mats of round, succulent, overlapping, bright-green leaves just 1/2-inch across on trailing stems, with the leaves overlapping on the stems like shingles or scales (the 'turtle vine' name suggests the scale-like leaf pattern). The plant is one of the most popular terrarium and vivarium plants due to its miniature scale, shade tolerance, and ease of care; it is also used as a hanging basket subject and in fairy garden designs. A purple-leaved cultivar ('Pink Lady') with pink-purple leaves is widely grown and striking. Turtle vine roots extremely easily — virtually any stem fragment with a node will root in moist soil. It is also commonly used as a supplemental diet food for many species of reptiles and tortoises, who eat the leaves. Virtually indestructible in humid shade conditions.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Foliage Plants

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade to full shade; Heucherella is a sterile bigeneric hybrid between Heuchera (coral bells) and Tiarella (foamflower) combining the bold, colorful foliage of heuchera with the spreading, ground-covering habit and foamy spring flowers of tiarella.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in part to full shade; mulch to maintain cool, moist roots. Heucherella tiarelloides (foamy bells or heucherella) is a sterile bigeneric hybrid between Heuchera (coral bells) and Tiarella (foamflower) — two closely related native North American shade perennials that cross naturally and have been extensively hybridized to produce a range of outstanding, foliage-forward shade perennials combining features of both parents. Heucherella plants produce the bold, often brilliantly colored and patterned foliage of heucheras (in purple, bronze, caramel, silver-and-green, lime green, and other combinations) combined with the ground-covering, stoloniferous spreading habit and foamy spring flower spikes of tiarella — an outstanding combination for shade gardening. Unlike their heuchera parents, many heucherella selections also show good heat tolerance. Modern named cultivars ('Sweet Tea,' 'Stoplight,' 'Tapestry,' 'Brass Lantern') offer striking foliage colors that change seasonally. In zone 9, heucherella performs best in shade with consistent moisture and protection from summer afternoon heat. An excellent companion for hostas, ferns, and astilbes in shade gardens.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Shade Perennials
